stories.story: The end of the 1960s arrived with the Rolling Stones' Altamont fiasco in December 1969, the Kent State shootings in May 1970, or Richard Nixon's 1972 re-election. For the show "Mad Men," the "end of an era" begins Sunday. "Mad Men" is about a 1960s advertising agency and its collision with changing times. The show has inspired fashion lines, cocktails, museum exhibits, and presidential references.
key_facts: 1960s end markers: Altamont (Dec 1969), Kent State (May 1970), Nixon re-election (1972)
"Mad Men" is a show about a 1960s advertising agency
"Mad Men" inspired fashion, cocktails, museum exhibits, and presidential references
explicit_unknowns: The specific Sunday the era begins

stories.story: Don Draper, the creative director played by Jon Hamm, has become a symbol of the times. "Mad Men" subjects have taken the show to heart. In March, a "Mad Men" bench was unveiled in front of New York's Time & Life Building. This is where the fictional firm of Sterling Cooper & Partners has its headquarters.
key_facts: Don Draper is played by Jon Hamm
A "Mad Men" bench was unveiled in March at New York's Time & Life Building
Sterling Cooper & Partners is headquartered at the Time & Life Building
explicit_unknowns: The specific year the bench was unveiled

stories.story: The end of a TV series like "Mad Men" brings risk. "The Sopranos," the former employer of "Mad Men" creator Matthew Weiner, divided fans with a cut-to-black finale. Conversely, "Mad Men's" former AMC stablemate, "Breaking Bad," was saluted for an almost perfect landing.
key_facts: Matthew Weiner created "Mad Men" and formerly worked on "The Sopranos"
"The Sopranos" had a cut-to-black finale
"Breaking Bad" was an AMC stablemate of "Mad Men"
explicit_unknowns: The specific risks associated with the "Mad Men" finale

stories.story: The first half of season 7 of "Mad Men" ended with the moon landing in July 1969. Matthew Weiner and his cast have been typically tight-lipped, with Matthew Weiner even hiding the finale from the cast. It is reasonable to assume the new season of "Mad Men" will pick up soon after July 1969.
key_facts: Season 7 first half ended with the July 1969 moon landing
Matthew Weiner hid the finale from the cast
explicit_unknowns: The exact date the new season picks up

stories.story: "Mad Men" probably won't jump ahead much from the end of 1969. The latter half of 1969 included the Manson murders, the Woodstock festival, a New York mayoral campaign, and the Vietnam War moratorium demonstrations. The show "Mad Men" might mention the Miracle Mets to acknowledge the agency's late Lane Pryce. Matthew Weiner, who is from Baltimore, might have a different idea.
key_facts: Late 1969 events: Manson murders, Woodstock, NY mayoral campaign, Vietnam War moratorium
Lane Pryce is a late character at the agency
Matthew Weiner is from Baltimore
explicit_unknowns: Whether the Miracle Mets will actually be mentioned

stories.story: "Mad Men" is generally a show about disintegration, reflective of the 1960s. White-shoe WASP firms like Sterling Cooper are giving way to ethnic pace-setters such as Doyle Dane Bernbach. Grimy New York is replaced by sunny Los Angeles, and the "Good War" generation butts heads with the "Make Love, Not War" cohort. Vacuum tubes and ledger books are being displaced by a sleek, solid-state IBM world.
key_facts: Sterling Cooper represents white-shoe WASP firms
Doyle Dane Bernbach represents ethnic pace-setters
IBM technology is displacing vacuum tubes and ledger books

stories.story: Sterling Cooper saw intraoffice turmoil due to a merger with former rival Cutler Gleason and Chaough. The agency is now without Bert Cooper (Robert Morse) and owned by Madison Avenue titan McCann Erickson. Longtime characters like Ken Cosgrove (Aaron Staton), Harry Crane (Rich Sommer), and Joan Harris (Christina Hendricks) might look for an exit.
key_facts: Sterling Cooper merged with Cutler Gleason and Chaough
The agency is owned by McCann Erickson
Bert Cooper is played by Robert Morse
Potential exits: Ken Cosgrove (Aaron Staton), Harry Crane (Rich Sommer), Joan Harris (Christina Hendricks)
explicit_unknowns: Why these specific characters would look for an exit

stories.story: Roger Sterling, the wisecracking executive played by John Slattery at Sterling Cooper, might find an exit. Roger Sterling has suffered two heart attacks and drinks to excess. Because Roger Sterling has never grown up, a sudden and shocking departure is possible.
key_facts: Roger Sterling is played by John Slattery
Roger Sterling had two heart attacks and drinks to excess
explicit_unknowns: The timing of the heart attacks

stories.story: Peggy Olson's star has continued to rise, similar to advertising wunderkind Mary Wells Lawrence. Peggy Olson left Sterling Cooper once and returned when her new agency merged with Sterling Cooper. If Peggy Olson, played by Elisabeth Moss, bolts the firm where Don Draper works, she might head her own agency or get married.
key_facts: Peggy Olson is played by Elisabeth Moss
Peggy Olson is compared to Mary Wells Lawrence
Peggy Olson previously left and returned to Sterling Cooper
explicit_unknowns: Whether Peggy Olson is still interested in marriage

stories.story: Don Draper's ex-wife, Betty (January Jones), has lost herself amid turmoil. Betty sees herself through her spouses, and though husband Henry Francis (Christopher Stanley) is supportive, he is a busy man. Sally, Don Draper's daughter (Kiernan Shipka), is a bright teenager starting to rebel. Sally has run away, started sneaking cigarettes, and fights with her mother, Betty.
key_facts: Betty is played by January Jones
Henry Francis is played by Christopher Stanley
Sally is played by Kiernan Shipka
explicit_unknowns: The specific nature of the turmoil Betty is facing

stories.story: Sally, the daughter of Don Draper, might hitchhike to Woodstock or drop out of school. Given what Sally has been through—divorces, affairs, office politics, and morose late-night rides with Glen Bishop—this might be too on the nose.
key_facts: Sally has had late-night rides with Glen Bishop
explicit_unknowns: Whether Sally actually goes to Woodstock

stories.story: On April 1, 1970, Richard Nixon might sign legislation banning cigarette ads on radio and television. This would remind Don Draper of the Lucky Strike campaign he created 10 years earlier. Don Draper might fix a drink, ponder an avocado-colored refrigerator, and clean his ashtray. Don Draper would leave the show on top of the zeitgeist, unable to accept his past and utterly, inscrutably alone.
key_facts: Prediction date: April 1, 1970
Richard Nixon banned cigarette ads on radio and TV
Don Draper created the Lucky Strike campaign 10 years prior
explicit_unknowns: Whether this specific sequence of events occurs in the actual finale
